text:
If you’re building with LLMs and keep thinking ‘someone must have already done this better’… you’re right.🚀

I just stumbled on the single best GitHub repo I’ve seen in 2026:
Awesome-llm-apps—a living, breathing collection of 70+ real, ready-to-run LLM applications packed with AI agents, RAG pipelines, multi-agent teams, voice agents, and everything in between.

We’re talking.

Starter agents that plan your next trip or roast your memes
Advanced multi-agent crews doing VC due diligence, fraud investigation, and legal reviews

Self-evolving agents that actually improve their own skills
Voice RAG agents, game-playing agents, MCP agents that control browsers and GitHub…

And deep dives into agentic RAG, memory, fine-tuning, and cost optimization

All of it using OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, xAI, or fully open-source models like Llama & Gemma.

104,000 stars. Updated literally hours ago. Every project has clean code, requirements, and a README that actually gets you running in minutes.
If you’re in AI, data, product, or just love building things that feel like magic… This repo is pure gold.

🔗 Grab it here before your next side project: Shubham Saboo
https://lnkd.in/ghrENSrT

Quick question for you:
What’s ONE LLM app or agent you’ve always wanted to build but never got around to? Drop it below. I might just clone it this weekend

Just read this Reddit post from r/csMajors and it stopped me cold. 🚀

A guy who got hired at Anthropic three weeks ago dropped this:
‘His team doesn’t write code anymore.
Nobody on the team has hand-written code in months.
They run multiple agents in parallel and act more like managers than engineers.’

The mental model shift he described is wild:
‘You’re not using AI to code faster.
You are the PM. The agents are your engineers.

Your only job is to keep them all unblocked.’
He called the productivity gap between people who’ve made this switch and people who haven’t ‘already enormous,’ and this is inside Anthropic, where everyone is fully bought into AI by default.
This isn’t some far-future vision. It’s happening right now in 2026.
I’ve been experimenting with exactly this kind of multi-agent orchestration in n8n (the news summarizer workflow I shared recently is just the beginning). Once you start spinning up agents instead of writing every line yourself, you genuinely feel the shift.
The question isn’t ‘Will AI replace engineers?’ anymore.

It’s ‘Are you still writing code… or are you directing the team that does?’
If you’re in tech, data, or product, where do you stand right now? Still grinding solo in the IDE, or already running your own agent fleet?
Drop your take below. 👇 I’m reading every comment.

Jensen Huang just dropped the ultimate AI wake-up call: ‘Top engineers should be using AI aggressively… not avoiding it.’

He’s not whispering it either; the Nvidia CEO says companies should be ready to spend AI tokens worth up to HALF an engineer’s salary. Why?

Because it can unlock 10× productivity and has quietly become one of the strongest recruiting levers in Silicon Valley.

Tokens aren’t some nerdy metric anymore. They’re being treated like salary, bonuses, and equity combined are the new currency of real output and competitive edge.

Sam Altman is right there with him, calling it ‘universal basic compute.’
This isn’t futurist talk. This is happening right now.

The game has changed: the teams (and people) who treat AI tokens as a core investment are pulling ahead fast.

So here’s the real question:
Are you (or your company) allocating for AI the way Silicon Valley already is… or still pretending it’s optional?

Drop your honest take below. 👇 I read every single one.

Ever sat in a meeting pretending you know what ‘LLM’, ‘Transformer’, or ‘GenAI’ actually means? 🙋‍♂️

Same.

This one chart just ended the confusion forever:

👉AI = the big umbrella (machines that think, learn & act human)
👉ML = teaches machines from data
👉Neural Networks = brain-inspired pattern spotters
👉Deep Learning = multi-layered magic for tough tasks
👉Transformers = context-understanding superstars
👉GenAI = creates instead of just analyzing
👉LLM = trained on mountains of text
👉GPT = text-generating beast
👉GPT-5.3 = newest & smartest version
👉ChatGPT = your everyday genius sidekick

Screenshot this. Save it. Never fake it again

Which term still gets you? Drop it below 👇

I finally understood how LangGraph works.

Before this, it felt confusing.

Everyone talks about agents, workflows, nodes, and edges.
But nothing really clicks.

⸻

Then I saw it like this.

A user says
‘My order is late’

Now instead of just replying,
The system actually tries to figure out what’s going on.

First step checks the order.
Then it decides why it’s late.
Restaurant issue or rider issue.

Based on that, it takes action.
Offer a refund or ping the rider.

And if it still doesn’t work,
It tries again.

⸻

The key idea is this.

Every step shares the same memory.

One step writes.
Next step reads.

That’s how the system knows what already happened.

⸻

In LangGraph terms:

The state is the shared memory.
Nodes are just functions.
Edges are the paths between decisions.

⸻

Once I saw it this way,
It stopped feeling like a framework.

It started feeling like a flow.

⸻

If you’re learning about AI agents,
This mental model helps a lot.

Save this.

This whiteboard means more to me than it looks.

There was a time I used to sit quietly in the back, watching others explain system design.

I understood some of it.
Pretended to understand the rest.

I never thought I’d be the one standing in front of a board, explaining AWS architectures to others.

Content creation changed that.

The first time I recorded a tech video, I was nervous.
The first time I explained cloud architecture publicly, I realized how much I didn’t know.

That feeling could’ve stopped me.

Instead, it pushed me.

Every time I hit ‘post,’ I knew I had to be better.
Clearer.
More honest.
More prepared.

Content didn’t just build an audience.

It built confidence.
It built clarity.
It built courage.

If you’re waiting to feel ready before you start sharing…
You’ll wait forever.

Start before you’re ready.
Grow in public.
Let the pressure shape you.

If someone wants to start creating educational content on Instagram,
these are a few things worth thinking about early.

Most advice around content creation sounds like:
→ ‘Post daily.’
→ ‘Be consistent.’
→ ‘Just explain well.’

That advice isn’t wrong but it’s often incomplete.

While creating short-form educational content over the last few months,
I tried to anchor my approach in learning science and how people actually consume information on these platforms.

A few patterns kept repeating:

• One idea per reel
Reducing cognitive load seems to improve comprehension and completion.

• Simple language matters
When content feels mentally expensive, people disengage quickly.

• Fundamentals reach more people than advanced topics
Most learners are still building their base.

• The first few seconds are critical
Relevance needs to be clear almost immediately.

• Examples accelerate understanding
Concrete scenarios consistently outperform abstract explanations.

I’ve been using these as guiding principles not rigid rules and observing how people respond.

The growth and engagement that followed feel less like the goal,
and more like a byproduct of designing for how people learn.

I’m continuing to refine this approach over the next 90 days.

Would genuinely love to hear from educators and experienced professionals here:
Which of these align with what you’ve seen work when teaching or explaining concepts?

I built a team of AI Researchers

Here's how the project works:

The system works through a five-agent pipeline, where each agent performs one specialised task and passes structured JSON to the next stage.

First, a Planner Agent receives the user topic and decomposes it into 4-6 precise academic search queries by reasoning across multiple research angles like foundational concepts, methods, benchmarks, applications, and open problems.

Next, a Search Agent executes these queries using Exa and Tavily neural search APIs, retrieving results from trusted domains such as arXiv, IEEE, ACL papers, GitHub repositories, and official documentation.
A Validator Agent then evaluates every source using a scoring rubric based on credibility, recency, and technical depth, keeping only the top five highest-quality references.

The Extractor Agent downloads the selected sources, parses PDFs or webpages, and extracts datasets, metrics, key findings, and verbatim quotes while enforcing strict token and character limits.

Finally, the Synthesizer Agent compiles this evidence into a structured Markdown research report with inline citations, ensuring every claim is grounded in real sources.
The result is an AI research pipeline that actually researches not just generates text.

Hoping this video helps you to generate more such cool projects!

Happy Coding!

AI isn’t your competition. The person using it is.

Most coding tools still force you into the same routine.

Plan → Build → Wait → Fix → Repeat.

You start something, wait for it to finish, then move to the next part.

That rhythm slows everything down.

Replit Agent 4 changes that.

Now you can plan, design, and build at the same time.

While one task is building the backend,
you can work on the UI on an infinite canvas.

At the same time, another task can handle a different feature or generate assets for the project.

Everything moves in parallel, so you don’t lose momentum.

What this means in practice:

1. Run multiple build tasks at once
2. Design and iterate visually while your app is being built
3. Create web apps, mobile apps, slides, data tools, and more in the same project
4. Work with your team while the system handles the heavy lifting

No jumping between tools.
No waiting for one step to finish before starting another.

Just ideas turning into working products much faster.

Creativity runs on Replit.

If you want to try it yourself:

The current AI wave is exciting, but also a little scary.

Do we even need to code anymore?
Which technologies should we focus on?
What should we actually be learning?
Will companies just replace engineers with a few AI tools?

Instead of overthinking all the noise, I’m taking on a 30-day challenge.

Over the next month, I’ll build a set of projects to better understand what AI engineers actually build and where the real opportunities are in this space.

If you want to take this challenge with me, comment below. Let’s build together.

Here’s the list of projects I’ll be building:

1. Autonomous Multi-Agent Research System
An AI system where multiple agents collaborate to research a topic, validate sources, extract insights, and generate a structured report with citations.

2. GitHub Codebase Intelligence System
A tool that ingests an entire GitHub repository and helps developers understand it by generating architecture summaries, explaining modules, and answering questions about the codebase.

3. AI System Design Interview Simulator
An AI interviewer that runs system design interviews, asks follow-up questions, evaluates architecture decisions, and gives structured feedback on missing components.

4. Autonomous Web Browsing Agent
An AI agent that can navigate websites, gather information, interact with pages, and complete tasks like comparing products or collecting data across multiple sources.

5. AI Knowledge Graph Builder
A system that ingests large document collections and automatically extracts entities and relationships to build an interactive knowledge graph with semantic search.

6. Self-Improving Prompt Optimization System
An AI system that automatically improves prompts by testing outputs, evaluating performance, mutating prompts, and selecting the best performing versions.

7. AI Data Analyst Agent
A tool where you upload a dataset and the AI performs analysis, finds correlations, generates visualizations, and explains insights in natural language.

8. AI Personal Memory System
A long-term memory engine that stores documents, notes, and research, enabling semantic search and knowledge recall across everything you’ve uploaded.

9. AI Startup Product Generator
An AI system that takes a startup idea and generates a product concept, roadmap, technical architecture, and go-to-market strategy.

10. AI Debugging Assistant
A system that analyzes a codebase, identifies bugs, traces root causes, and suggests fixes for errors developers encounter.

30 days. 10 projects. Let’s see where this goes.

I used to think my problem was not using AI enough.

Turns out, my problem was using too many tools that didn’t remember anything about me. I’d jump between meetings → notes → docs → ChatGPT → Notion → Slack and still ask myself:

‘Wait, where did we discuss this?’
‘What was the exact number?’
‘Didn’t someone mention this last month?’

Most AI tools help you respond better. They don’t really help you retain context.

I recently came across TwinMind while exploring tools focused more on memory than generation. What stood out was that it doesn’t start from scratch every time. It keeps track of past meetings, calls, and conversations. It makes decisions, highlights, and follow-ups searchable later, not just transcripts.

That makes a real difference in roles where losing context is more expensive than not having ideas. It’s not a replacement for ChatGPT. It feels like a different category altogether. AI as working memory, not just a response engine.

When I reached out to the TwinMind team to share my feedback, they genuinely appreciated it and mentioned that they see their users as winners who deserve the best. They shared a few TwinMind Pro account accesses with me to pass along.

If this resonates, comment ‘ME + your name’ below.
They want to know who they’re building for.

Sharing this in case it helps anyone thinking about how AI fits into everyday work beyond prompting.

The Psychology behind Vision Boards (And Why Most People Use Them Wrong)

Vision boards don’t work because the universe is listening.
They work because your brain is.

When done right, vision boards align your attention, emotions, belief system, and actions toward a goal. When done wrong, they turn into aesthetic Pinterest collages that make you feel good but do nothing.

Here’s what’s really happening.

1. Vision Boards Train Your Attention
Your brain uses something called the Reticular Activating System (RAS) to decide what deserves attention. When you create a vision board, you’re telling your brain: this matters.

For example, if you add ‘AI research’ or ‘startup work’ to your board, you suddenly notice relevant LinkedIn posts, blogs, people, and opportunities. They didn’t magically appear, you just stopped filtering them out.

2. They Boost Motivation Through Emotion
Emotionally charged images trigger dopamine, the motivation chemical. This is why seeing visuals of goals you care about feels energising. Your brain remembers emotional goals better than abstract to-do lists.

For example, an image of someone coding late at night sticks more than the text ‘practice daily’.

3. They Build Self-Belief
Repeated visualisation strengthens self-efficacy, the belief that you can actually do this. Your brain treats vivid mental rehearsal as practice, making real action feel more familiar and less intimidating.

4. They Work Only When Paired With Action
This is where most people fail. Visualising only the outcome (money, success, abs) can reduce effort, a phenomenon called fantasy depletion.

The fix?
Visualise the process. Not just ‘dream job’, but also:

- Studying daily
- Writing applications
- Getting rejected and trying again

How to Build an Effective Vision Board
- Include process + outcome images
- Keep it personal, realistic, and visible
- Review weekly and track real progress

PS: sharing my vision board below for 2026. Share yours in the comments below :)

I cracked 9 internships in 2 years, not because I was smarter.

For a long time, I was stuck doing frontend and full-stack internships. I was learning, but it felt repetitive. I wanted to move into ML and AI, yet every application felt like it disappeared into the void.

So I stopped applying blindly and changed my approach.

I started an AI blog out of pure curiosity. I wrote about complex ML concepts in simple language, shared code implementations, and documented how I thought through problems. I posted these blogs on LinkedIn, built a small newsletter, and added everything to my resume.

Two people noticed.
An AI startup CEO offered me a data science internship.
An IISc professor turned one blog into a project and then an internship.

That’s when I understood proof of work.

Proof of work is demo videos explaining your projects, blogs showing your thinking, GitHub repos with clean READMEs, case studies instead of buzzwords, and a resume that links everything together.

Sometimes, you don’t need to be the smartest person in the room, just the most visible one.

ps: my financial situation can only allow me to share these tips as presents this year, Happy New Year everyone :))

Most developers think AI coding tools are about writing code faster.

That’s only the surface.

The real shift is that AI changes how software gets built.

Instead of spending hours on boilerplate, setup, debugging simple errors, or searching docs, developers can move their time toward architecture, product thinking, and solving harder problems.

The best engineers are starting to work differently.

They use AI to:

- Generate first drafts of features
n- Refactor messy code into cleaner patterns
- Explain unfamiliar codebases quickly
- Write tests and edge cases
- Turn ideas into prototypes in hours instead of weeks
- Learn new frameworks while building

But there’s an important truth.

AI doesn’t replace coding knowledge.
It amplifies it.

If you understand systems, logic, and clean engineering principles, AI becomes a force multiplier.
If you don’t, it can generate confusion faster.

The future developer may write fewer lines manually, but they’ll need stronger judgment than ever.

Knowing what to build.
Knowing what good code looks like.
Knowing when the AI is wrong.
Knowing how to ship real products.

Code is not disappearing.
The role of the coder is evolving.

The people who learn to direct AI well will have an unfair advantage.

This is only the beginning.

Most people learn Linear Regression as just another machine learning algorithm.

But it’s much more important than that.

Linear Regression is where many people first understand how data can predict outcomes.

At its core, it tries to find the best straight line through data points.
That line helps explain the relationship between inputs and outputs.

For example:

- Study hours → Exam score
- House size → Price
- Ad spend → Sales
- Experience → Salary

Instead of guessing patterns manually, the model learns the trend mathematically.

Why it matters:

- Easy to understand and explain
- Fast to train even on large datasets
- Strong baseline for prediction problems
- Shows which features influence results
- Builds intuition for advanced ML models

But there’s an important truth.

Linear Regression works best when relationships are roughly linear.
If data is noisy, highly complex, or non-linear, accuracy can drop.

That’s why real analysts don’t just run the model.
They check assumptions, clean data, remove outliers, and evaluate performance.

What makes it powerful is not complexity.
It’s clarity.

It teaches you how variables connect.
How prediction works.
How models learn from examples.

Before neural networks and advanced AI,
understanding Linear Regression builds the foundation.

Sometimes the simplest model teaches the deepest lessons.
